speaker: interviewer
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Today is February 11th, 1996. We're interviewing Dr. Helen N. Fagin, previously Neimark. Interviewer is Judith Richardson Haimes. We're in Sarasota, Florida in the United States, and the language is English. I'm Judith Richardson Haimes, H-A-I-M-E-S. We are conducting the interview today with Dr. Helen Fagin. Today's date is February 11th, 1996, and we are in Sarasota, Florida in the United States. We are conducting the interview in English. Dr. Fagin, can you tell us what your life was like in Radomsko, Poland before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was part of a middle-class Jewish family consisting of my father, my mother, and my older sister, and later on my younger sister, who came along later on. And we live in a town of Radomsko, which counted probably about 30,000 to 33,000 inhabitants, 10% of whom were Jews. The Jewish community was a very close community. It seems to me that everybody knew everybody. But it was also a community which suffered from some kind of class division. Middle-class people were, of course, the better-to-do people. These were the better-to-do merchants. And then there were the artisans, who did not fare as well. This really mostly manifested itself in the opportunities that we had, most especially in educational opportunities. Now, as long back as I remember, I never went to public schools. I went to private schools. And there were two reasons for it. One, there were no schools of high school level which were public schools that were open to Jews, so the necessity of educating the children was to create a private school, private high school. And because public schools were really not very keen on having Jewish students and Jewish pupils, that private school extended itself to the lowest grades as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember going straight from a kindergarten into this Jewish private school from which I matriculated with a degree, with a high school degree, which was called matura, M-A-T-U-R-A, which obviously connoted maturation. The education that I received in this Jewish high school, which was called gymnasium-- and because it was a private school, it was under the direction of a lady whose name was Ludwiga Weintraub. And the actual name of the school was Jewish Gymnasium by Ludwiga Weintraub.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very, very good professors, very good. I say professors because they were of professorial caliber. And the teaching there was on a very high level. I received, for example, my education was in all possible subject matter, including five years of German, five years of Latin and Hebrew. So that was-- and-- and I studied all kinds of subject, geometry and algebra and psychology and biology and zoology and geography, and, of course, Polish history, Polish literature, and such. We grew up-- our home was in the center of the town, which used to call-- to be called Rynek, 'rynek' meaning in Polish 'market,' because the marketplace was that square which was Rynek. It was the main square. Later it was renamed the Square of the 3rd of May, 3rd of May being a very important date in Polish history because Poles received their first constitution as a republic on the 3rd of May. We lived-- our address was Number 9, the Square, or Platz in Polish, the Square of the 3rd May, or Platz Tazhchego Maya, in-- in Radomsko. My father owned the house. It was a large corner apartment house. We had a department store on the ground floor and we occupied the upstairs home. Our house was very well appointed. We had a private stairway going up to the apartment. We had two entrance to the apartment, one leading to a kitchen and one to a den, which was my father's library, which was quite extensive. We had a very, very lovely living room. I can remember the appointments in our home. We had oriental rugs, which stayed only for the winter. And they were replaced-- put in mothballs, and replaced by lighter rugs in the summer. We had speciall- special draperies for the winter, which were replaced with lace curtains in the summer. We had some very, very special furniture, which I understand was made to order for my parents when they got married. It was all in mahogany, a massive mahogany table. I remember it was covered in a Gobelin tablecloth in the wintertime. In the summertime, it was replaced with a lace tablecloth. I remember a beautiful chandelier in that room. And I remember the beautiful tall vase that always stood on the table. We had a mahogany-- shiny mahogany piano. We had a tall mahogany grandfather's clock and a beautiful credenza. And this credenza held some very fine china. My mother had a set of Rosenthal china in that credenza. I remember very well the pattern. It was cobalt and gold pattern of that Rosenthal china. Beautiful crystalware and lovely silver. I remember we had a table standing there, which was there just to receive a silver set of tea service and coffee service. And a 4-foot sterling silver candelabra, which stood near-- near the entrance to the living room. And that candelabra served us, of course, as a Hanukkah candelabra. My father's library, or the den, was very unusual. It was known in the town that my father owned the largest library in town. He collected-- he was a scholar. He collected literature in many, many languages. I remember his collection of periodicals from very early years of their publication. One was which was a Hebrew periodical, and one was And he had them all leather-bound, and he always referred to them. He was a Talmudic scholar, so he had the Talmuds, which are very, very large in size, so he had a special shelf for the Talmudic books. He had books in Russian and German and Polish. And one of the things that I remember very vividly is that he had George Eliot's book in Hebrew translation. I remember his books on Nietzsche, on philosophers, and, of course, all the books that he could collect in Yiddish and in Hebrew. He was especially fond of the poetry of Hayim Nahman Bialik, and he was very fond of Ahad Ha'am. Ahad Ha'am was the first Jewish writer who decided to use the Hebrew language and revived the Hebrew language as a literary language in the 19th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not only was-- did he enjoy being a scholar, but others enjoyed his scholarship as well. I recall that once a month he had meetings in his house of a number of people, usually four or five, among whom were two Catholic priests and one judge, who were obviously not Jewish, and who came there just to take advantage of his scholarship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming through the house, going through the house, I also remember the bedrooms. My mother's and father's bedroom had a special attraction to me for two reasons. My mother stored there her beautiful clothes. And when I was a young girl, I liked to go in and try on some of her clothes. She had her engagement gown, which was a pale blue, satin blue, trimmed in ermine fur, and I remember dressing up in it. And the second thing that I remember about this bedroom, that she had custom-made furniture. And it was inlaid in little pieces of mirror and mother of pearl around it. And it was a very beautiful kind of hand-crafted furniture. And of course, there were the children's bedrooms that were not exceptional, just bedrooms. But our kitchen was really very interesting, because my-- my mother prided herself on being a very good homemaker. And we had a very large kitchen, I would say at least 20 by 20. And what very unusual about the kitchen was that it had mahogany floors that were constantly pasted and shined with a-- with a special machine. And there my mother would cook and bake. And in the kitchen, we had, of course, all the service, the china service. We had a big credenza that held two sets, of course, of dishes, one for meat dishes and one for dairy dishes, and the same thing with-- with silverware. And we had all kinds of preparation tables for baking and cooking, a stove, which was-- which was a coal stove, and a bin, which had a surface for baking, which that bin held the coal for the stove. And a bed, and the bed was for our maid, and she would sleep in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to that school I mentioned, and most of our friends were the children who actually went to the same school. And they were children of, again, I must mention this, of middle-class merchants. And the reason that there was a class distinction is because the others could not afford the education. And that was something which I still feel was very sad for our Jewish community, because some of the Jewish children could not avail themselves of the good education that some of us did. Our--our relationships were really connected mostly-- the children were mostly friendly through school and schoolwork and the school activities, and also through the relationships that my parents had with their friends. My parents would get together with their friends, mostly during the holidays and on the Shabbat. Shabbat was a visiting day. People would take a walk and they would visit each other. One-- that was very, very curious and very interesting. One did not have to announce one's visit. One just took a walk and knock at the door. And if the people were inside, they were welcomed. And whatever was in the house, they would be served some fresh fruit and coffee, tea, or whatever. And on the Shabbat, of course, hot drinks were not served, because the stove would not be ignited.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we would be friends with our parents' friends' children. And it would be a very close, lovely community, which provided us with these-- with these relationship. Of course, school was very important. Our entire life centered around school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Homework was important. We would get together doing homework together. We would study for exams together. And that would bind us together in very, very close relationships. And my sister, older sister Fela, was older than I-- than I was, and she was two grades ahead of me. And she had her circle of-- of friends who were a little more advanced and a little bit more mature and a little bit more outgoing than I was. I was very much involved in my schoolwork and reading and studying. Really that was my-- my main preoccupation. In the summers, I remember-- that was before our youngest sister was born-- we would go with my mother. We would go away to a resort place. One I remember was Ciechocinek, which was a sort of a health place where people would go and drink the health water. And the parents would just take their children along. But we also would be sent, my older sister and I would be sent to a-- it wasn't a camp. We called it a pension in the mountains, in the Tatra Mountains and the Carpathian Mountains, where we would go for the summer. And our parents would come to visit us on Parents' Day. They would sometimes vacation in an adult resort place called Krynica, and on the way they would visit with us. Later on, after Tobcia was born, who we call now Terry, we would go to a nearby place, which was sort of a-- a little country place, very, very nice, with a lot of forestry. And we would go there for the summer. And my father would come for the weekend and commute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you interact with any of the Gentile children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not interact with any of the Gentile children, because the social intercourse was just not something that we could enter into. My father and my mother knew a lot of the Gentile people, because they were our customers, but there was no social contact. Certainly neither would their parents, the Gentile parents, allow it, nor would we be comfortable with it. For example, there were two men's high school, boys' high schools, which were non-Jewish, Gentile high schools, and one girls' high school. And none of the Jewish children would be allowed to enter these high schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in as much as there wasn't overt anti-Semitism, as far as somebody calling us names, we knew our place. We knew that we couldn't enter into any kind of relationship. That changed later on when I went for a very brief stint at the-- to the university in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- the Jewish community was very much a tight community, but it was a very Jewish community. For example, without exception, whether persons-- Jewish people, may have been very religious or not religious at all, on Friday night at sunset, all Jewish establishments would close. All the stores would absolutely close and reopen Saturday night after sundown. No matter what kind of business they would have anticipated, this was something that was adhered to. And that, of course, took place also during the holidays. Not only would the stores be closed, but all the artisans would stop working. A tailor or a-- or a-- any-- any other artisan would stop working, because on a Shabbat a Jew does not work. He doesn't necessarily have to go to the house of prayer, but he just doesn't work. My father went to the house of prayer. My father was an ardent Zionist. But he was also a Mizrachi Zionist, which means he was a religious Zionist. And he would-- on a Friday night after he would close his store, he would change his clothes, and he would go into a little synagogue, which is called-- was called shtiebel. And there are very many shtiebels that different people frequented. My father would go to the shtiebel on a Friday night. And my mother and the children would go upstairs, got themselves ready for the Shabbat, and the table would be set. And we would wait for our father to come home, and then we would have the Shabbat meal. And the Shabbat meal was always a very traditional meal. As a matter of fact, on a Friday, my mother, who would help my father usually in the store-- on a Friday morning she would go upstairs. And even though we had one Polish maid who would clean and take care of the house-- there was a time that we also had a Jewish cook-- but my mother would always prepare the Shabbat. And for the Shabbat, we always had fish, and we always had chicken soup and some meat or chicken. And on Friday, very, very often my mother would make a special dish, a Jewish dish, which is called cholent. It is-- in English, it is spelled C-H-O-L-E-N-T. A cholent is a very traditional dish stemming from the fact that Jews would not cook on a Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they would take a-- a cast iron pot, and they would put good pieces of meat and potatoes and all kinds of beans and all kinds of different spices. And they would cover it with parchment paper, put a lid on it, and take it to a baker's oven. And they would leave it in the baker's oven, which would remain there unattended until the following morning, where we would retrieve the pot. And we would bring it home and then serve it as a meal. And it is absolutely delicious. It was very traditional.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, and, of course, the baking of the challah. I remember my mother was very, very careful in teaching us how to prepare the Shabbat and the intricate way of-- of baking the challah and folding the challah pieces, braiding the challah. And of course, we learned how to prepare the fish, the gefilte fish, which were different from the gefilte fish we know now in America. We actually stuffed-- 'gefilte' means 'stuffed.' And we actually stuffed the fish. We would retain the-- the skin, and we would remove the meat. And we would chop it up with all kinds of stuff and then stuff it. And that is why we call it gefilte, or stuffed fish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents ever with you for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know my grandparents. My grandparents must have died before I was born. I know of my grandparents. I know of my parents' siblings. But we have never-- I was named after my mother's mother. My older sister was named after my father's mother. And my younger sister was named after my mother's grandmother. So we have not known our grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other relatives that were close by?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my mother did not come from Radomsko. My mother came from Lodz, or 'wudge' in Polish. So all her siblings were mostly in Lodz. She had five sisters, I believe, and two brothers. Thanks to one brother who left Poland very, very early-- I think right after World War I he left for England. Thanks to his leaving Poland at that time, I have a lot of my own relatives, first and second cousins, in England now. So my mother had several sisters, two of whom migrated to Israel in the 1930s. And thanks to them, we have a lot of family in Israel, because my mother actually helped her two sisters and their children financially to leave Poland, and they actually settled in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: When were you first aware, if at all, of any anti-Semitism in your town or of any danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always aware of anti-Semitism. We knew anti-Semitism existed. We knew that in as much as it may not have been felt every day, it was there. It was covert, and it was overt in certain things. We knew that certain-- certain people were very anti-Semitic, that they didn't want to tolerate Jews in their town. But they simply co-existed. They simply co-existed. My-- my understanding of anti-Semitism was almost part of my life. We knew the reason we went to a Jewish school is because they wouldn't let us go to a non-- to a Gentile school. We knew the reason that we had to stay indoors, for example, at the time of religious processions, Catholic religious processions, is because we didn't want to incite the non-Jews. We knew, for example, that when there was a non-Jewish, a Gentile, funeral, for example, we would try to stay away because we are not-- we are not desired. Our presence would not be desired there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were certain-- some-- some were even superstitious elements into-- inculcated into us. But we knew that they existed. And we knew that that was part of our lives. And we just accepted it, because this was our modus vivendi. We had no other choice. When did you first realize that you were surrounded by danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I would like to track back a little bit. I-- I went to the university, to the Jagiellonian University in Krakow, to study after I was graduated from high school. And the first thing I knew, that I couldn't apply to any-- to-- to any department I wanted. I knew-- and I knew that many of my friends and my sister's friends who wanted to become doctors and lawyers had no chance of studying in Poland. Therefore, many, many of the people that I knew would go to France or would go to Belgium, for example. A friend of mine went to Belgium to study engineering. My sister's friends went to Paris to-- to study medicine or to study dentistry. I mean, in Poland, we could not gain access to any of these professional schools. You couldn't study law or any such thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I finally was able to gain admittance to the faculty of chemistry. My father, who was a great Zionist and who traveled a number of times in the 1930s to Israel, had the dream that I would eventually go to the Weizmann Institute in Rehovot. At that time, it wasn't named Weizmann. I think it was Fisk Institute or such thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, he-- he sort of-- in a ceremonious way, he put down my name. And he says, you go and study chemistry here. And then when we are ready to leave-- and indeed he was going to sell his business, and we were all going to move to Israel. And that he planned to do in 1940, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, when I went to the university in Krakow, I realized I couldn't sit on the right side. I had to stand-- to sit on the left side, because I was Jewish. We couldn't wear-- we were not allowed to wear the insignia of the university. There were certain caps that you would wear showing that you are a university student. We could not wear it. And in certain auditoria, we had standing room only. So this hit me immediately, and I knew that we were second-class citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- to answer your question, when I knew the danger existed was in August of 1939, when we saw certain kind of signs, politically and militarily and otherwise. And that is when we decided that we better think how to prepare for it and worry about what may come.
